South Asian Traditions

Umāsvāti

c. 2nd – 4th c. CE

Jīva, karma, cognition, and liberation systematized into one of Jainism’s foundational philosophical maps.

The image above is a later devotional representation, not an historical portrait. The report below is text-based.

Umāsvāti’s Philosophy of Mind: A Reconstruction from the Tattvārthasūtra

1. Central thesis

Umāsvāti’s philosophy of mind is not a “philosophy of mind” in the modern disciplinary sense, because the Tattvārthasūtra is a liberation-oriented metaphysical, epistemological, ethical, and cosmological treatise. But it contains a systematic theory of consciousness, cognition, perception, error, embodiment, affect, agency, mental discipline, and perfected knowledge. Reconstructed in contemporary terms, its core thesis is this:

The true subject is not the mind. The true subject is the soul, jīva. The soul’s defining mark is upayoga: active, attentive, cognitive consciousness. Mind, senses, body, speech, and karmic matter are not the subject itself; they are instruments, conditions, channels, and obstructions through which the embodied soul’s cognition becomes partial, sequential, distorted, and morally entangled. Liberation is the removal of those obstructions, culminating in omniscient, non-instrumental cognition.

That makes Umāsvāti’s position a form of pluralist, realist, non-reductive, karmic substance-dualism, but not Cartesian dualism. It is dualist because conscious souls and non-conscious matter are irreducibly distinct. It is pluralist because there are many souls, not one universal mind. It is realist because external substances and their modes are real. It is karmic because mental, affective, verbal, and bodily activity causally conditions the soul’s bondage to subtle matter. It is soteriological because the highest form of mind is not ordinary subjectivity but purified, unobstructed knowledge.

2. Historical and textual position

The Tattvārthasūtra is traditionally associated with Umāsvāti or Umāsvāmin. Modern scholarship treats the authorship and dating cautiously: the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes the text as associated with Umāsvāmin and “probably written by multiple authors around 150–400 CE,” while also identifying it as the first major representative of classical Jaina philosophy and the oldest extant Jaina treatise in Sanskrit. Britannica likewise describes it as an early Common Era Jain work, claimed by both major Jain communities, and as the first Jain philosophical work in Sanskrit to address logic, epistemology, ontology, ethics, cosmography, and cosmogony. For the purposes of this report, “Umāsvāti” refers to the Tattvārthasūtra and its immediate doctrinal horizon, while recognizing that the text is terse and later commentaries often make explicit what the sūtras compress.

Its historical significance is considerable. In Indian philosophy, Umāsvāti stands at the point where Jain thought becomes a systematic Sanskrit philosophical project, capable of direct comparison with Nyāya, Sāṃkhya, Buddhist Abhidharma, early Vedānta, and later Jain logicians. The Tattvārthasūtra begins not with psychology in the modern sense, but with the path to liberation: right view, right knowledge, and right conduct. It then grounds that path in seven basic categories: soul, non-soul, influx of karmic matter, bondage, stoppage, shedding, and liberation.

This already tells us something decisive: for Umāsvāti, the mind cannot be understood apart from metaphysics and ethics. Cognition is not merely a psychological event. It is part of the soul’s ontological condition and part of its progress or regress within saṃsāra.

3. A methodological caution: “mind” is not one thing here

A modern reader may look for a term equivalent to “mind.” That is misleading. Umāsvāti’s system distributes what modern philosophy calls “mind” across several terms:

Jīva is the conscious subject, the living soul.

Upayoga is the soul’s characteristic manifestation as cognition, attention, or active consciousness.

Jñāna is knowledge or determinate cognition.

Darśana is perception or indeterminate awareness, distinct from jñāna in Jain epistemology.

Manas is “mind” in a narrower sense: an internal instrument or quasi-sense involved in cognition, especially scriptural or linguistic cognition.

Indriya are the senses, understood both physically and psychically.

Yoga is activity of body, speech, and mind; in Jain karma theory it is the channel through which karmic matter flows toward the soul.

Kaṣāya are passions such as anger, pride, deceit, and greed, which bind the soul to karma.

So the Jain philosophy of mind extracted from Umāsvāti is not a theory of “the mind” as a single inner theater. It is a theory of the conscious soul, its instruments, its obstructions, its affective distortions, and its possible purification.

4. Ontology: a psycho-physical pluralism

Umāsvāti’s world is built from living and non-living realities. The seven tattvas are jīva, ajīva, āsrava, bandha, saṃvara, nirjarā, and mokṣa: soul, non-soul, influx, bondage, stoppage, shedding, and liberation. A later translation with Pūjyapāda’s commentary glosses jīva as characterized by consciousness and ajīva as having characteristics opposite to the soul.

This is not idealism. Non-conscious reality is real. Matter, space, the media of motion and rest, and, in some traditions, time, are real substances. Matter is not a mere appearance in consciousness. Nor is the system materialist, because consciousness is not a product or property of matter. Consciousness is the distinctive mark of jīva. The result is a metaphysics of irreducible conscious and non-conscious substances.

This metaphysics is also not static. The Tattvārthasūtra defines existence through origination, destruction, and permanence: substances persist while their modes change. This is crucial for mind. It lets Umāsvāti say that the soul remains the same enduring subject while its cognitive, affective, karmic, and embodied modes vary. Without this substance-mode distinction, transmigration, moral responsibility, memory, and liberation would be difficult to explain.

A striking difference from later Cartesian dualism is that the Jain soul is not an unextended point of mentality. The soul is spatially related to the body; it is described as occupying space like lamplight and as contracting or expanding according to embodiment. This means Jain dualism does not divide reality into unextended thinking substance and extended matter in the Cartesian manner. The soul is non-material, but it has spatial “space-points” or pradeśas and is coextensive with its embodied condition.

This gives Umāsvāti a distinctive solution to the mind-body problem. The soul and body are not identical, but neither are they sealed off in separate metaphysical compartments. They coexist, interpenetrate in bondage, and interact through karmic and material conditions. Matter forms the basis of the body, speech, mind, and respiration, and matter also contributes to pleasure, suffering, life, and death.

5. The definition of the conscious subject: upayoga

The central sūtra for the philosophy of mind is Tattvārthasūtra 2.8: upayogo lakṣaṇam. In the cited translation: “Cognition, upayoga, is the mark or distinctive characteristic of the soul.” Pūjyapāda’s commentary glosses upayoga as “active or attentive consciousness” and explicitly says that it distinguishes the soul from the body.

This is the closest Jain analogue to a criterion of mentality. But it is stronger than a mere criterion. Upayoga is not simply one feature the soul happens to have. It is the soul’s defining manifestation. To be a jīva is to be capable of conscious cognitive engagement. The body may be living in a biological sense, but what makes it genuinely animate is the presence of jīva.

Upayoga has two principal forms: jñānopayoga, the manifestation of knowledge, and darśanopayoga, the manifestation of perception or indeterminate awareness. The Tattvārthasūtra says upayoga is twofold, with further subdivisions; the Jain tradition then distinguishes forms of knowledge such as sensory knowledge, scriptural knowledge, clairvoyance, telepathy, and omniscience, as well as forms of perception.

This yields a sophisticated theory of consciousness. Consciousness is not exhausted by discursive thought. It includes both determinate knowing and a more basic perceptual or presentational awareness. In modern terms, Jain thought distinguishes something like object-presenting awareness from conceptually articulated cognition, though the mapping is not exact. Darśana is not simply “sense perception” in the Western empiricist sense, and jñāna is not merely propositional belief.

There is also a built-in theory of attention. Upayoga, as active or attentive consciousness, implies that the soul’s cognitive manifestation is not inert. Ordinary embodied souls manifest cognition sequentially and partially. Omniscient beings, by contrast, know all substances and all modes simultaneously.

6. Knowledge and pramāṇa: a non-empiricist epistemology of mind

Umāsvāti’s theory of knowledge is central to his philosophy of mind because knowledge is not merely a mental state. It is the soul’s own manifestation under conditions of greater or lesser obstruction.

The Tattvārthasūtra identifies five kinds of knowledge: mati-jñāna or sensory/empirical knowledge, śruta-jñāna or scriptural/verbal knowledge, avadhi-jñāna or clairvoyant knowledge, manaḥparyaya-jñāna or direct knowledge of others’ mental states, and kevala-jñāna or omniscience.

The text then makes a move that sharply distinguishes Jain epistemology from many Western empiricist assumptions. The first two, sensory and scriptural knowledge, are classified as indirect, because they depend on other factors: senses, mind, light, teaching, and other external causes. The remaining three are direct, because they arise in the soul without dependence on the senses.

This is philosophically important. In much Western philosophy, especially after early modern empiricism, sense perception is often treated as the paradigm of direct acquaintance with the world. For Umāsvāti, ordinary sense-based cognition is not the highest form of directness; it is mediated by instruments. True directness is non-instrumental manifestation in the soul. This reverses the expected hierarchy. The more cognition depends on bodily and mental instruments, the less direct it is. The more the soul knows by its own purified capacity, the more direct it is.

The theory of pramāṇa is also distinctive. The cited translation of Tattvārthasūtra 1.10 says the five kinds of knowledge are pramāṇas, and the commentary rejects the view that sense-object contact or the senses themselves are pramāṇa. Knowledge itself is the valid cognition. The commentary also uses the lamp analogy: knowledge illuminates its object and its own nature, avoiding a regress in which one cognition would require another cognition to know it.

That gives Umāsvāti’s tradition a form of reflexive epistemology. Knowledge is not a hidden state that needs a second-order observer to reveal it. In its valid form, it is self-manifesting and object-manifesting. This is not identical to later phenomenology, but it converges with a central phenomenological intuition: consciousness is not merely a causal state inside an organism; it has a first-personal luminosity.

7. Sensory cognition, memory, recognition, and inference

Umāsvāti does not reduce cognition to sensation. Sensory knowledge, mati-jñāna, is broad. The tradition includes within it processes such as sensory cognition, remembrance, recognition, induction, and deduction. It also describes stages of sensory cognition: initial impression, inquiry, comprehension, and retention.

This is a compact but rich cognitive model. It distinguishes initial contact-like awareness from questioning, determination, and retention. It recognizes that cognition is temporally structured. Perception is not merely passive reception; it develops into discrimination, memory, and inferential orientation.

The Jain model therefore has resources for what contemporary philosophers would call perceptual processing, attention, concept formation, memory, and judgment. But it embeds all of these in a metaphysics of the soul and karma rather than in a neural or computational framework.

Error is also handled in a moral-epistemic way. Tattvārthasūtra 1.31 says sensory, scriptural, and clairvoyant cognition can also be erroneous; the commentary links such error to wrong belief, mithyādarśana.

This is not merely the claim that people can make perceptual mistakes. It is the deeper claim that cognition can be distorted by the soul’s orientation. False worldview, passion, and karmic obscuration are not external to epistemology; they are conditions of epistemic failure. In modern language, Umāsvāti offers a theory in which affective, ethical, and doxastic corruption alters the subject’s access to reality.

8. Manas: mind as instrument, not subject

One of the most important distinctions in Umāsvāti is the distinction between jīva and manas. The soul is the conscious subject. Manas is an instrument or internal cognitive organ. This prevents a common mistranslation: Jainism does not identify the soul with “mind” if by mind one means manas.

Sensory knowledge is caused by the senses and the mind. Yet the commentary says the soul is of the nature of knowledge and that the senses function as instruments through which the soul acquires knowledge under karmic conditions.

The senses themselves are divided into physical and psychical senses. The psychical sense consists of labdhi, attainment produced by the destruction-cum-subsidence of knowledge-obscuring karma, and upayoga, active consciousness.

This shows that sense perception is not merely anatomical. A working organ is not sufficient. There must also be a psychical capacity and a karmic condition that permits cognition. The physical organ, the psychical sense, the mind, the soul’s upayoga, and the karmic state all cooperate.

Manas has a special relation to scriptural or linguistic cognition. Tattvārthasūtra 2.21 says scriptural knowledge is the province of the mind; the commentary adds that the mind assists cognition, that the senses cannot function in their own domains without it, and that mind can independently accomplish scriptural cognition.

This gives Umāsvāti a layered model of cognition:

The soul is the subject.

Upayoga is conscious manifestation.

Senses provide modality-specific instruments.

Manas coordinates, assists, and supports scriptural or conceptual cognition.

Karma conditions what can be known and how clearly.

Matter supplies the embodied basis of body, speech, mind, and respiration.

The result is neither crude dualism nor crude materialism. It is an instrumental dualism: the conscious subject is non-material, but embodied cognition requires material and psychical instruments.

9. Embodiment and the body-mind relation

Umāsvāti’s account of embodiment is more intricate than the standard “soul versus body” formula suggests. Matter, pudgala, forms the basis of body, speech, mind, and respiration. Matter also contributes to pleasure, pain, life, and death.

This means the Jain soul is not a disembodied spectator during ordinary life. Embodied cognition depends on material organization. The body, speech, and mind are not illusions; they are karmically structured material supports for the soul’s activity.

Still, the soul is not reducible to them. The commentary on upayoga explicitly says cognition distinguishes soul from body even when soul and body are bound together.

This is where Umāsvāti differs both from physicalism and from simple substance dualism. Physicalism, in slogan form, is the thesis that everything is physical; modern functionalism, by contrast, identifies mental states by causal role within a cognitive system rather than by their intrinsic material constitution. Umāsvāti accepts neither view: mental life is causally structured and instrumentally embodied, but consciousness is not identical with the body, brain, causal role, or internal organ.

The closest modern label would be non-reductive embodied soul-realism. But that label still misses the karmic dimension. For Umāsvāti, embodiment is not just biological embodiment; it is karmically mediated embodiment.

10. Karma as a theory of mental obstruction

The philosophy of mind in Umāsvāti cannot be separated from karma. Karma is not simply moral bookkeeping. In Jainism it is subtle matter that binds to the soul through activity and passion. Tattvārthasūtra 8.1 lists wrong belief, non-abstinence, negligence, passions, and activity as causes of bondage. Tattvārthasūtra 8.2 says the living being, actuated by passions, retains particles of matter fit to become karma.

The mechanics of bondage are tied to yoga, defined as activity of body, speech, and mind. This activity is the influx, āsrava, because it functions like a conduit for karmic matter into the soul.

Passion determines the karmic seriousness of activity. Activity with passions causes karmic influx that extends transmigration, while activity without passions has a different, non-binding or less binding character.

This is a powerful moral psychology. The same outward behavior can have different karmic and mental significance depending on the passion or non-passion behind it. Cognition, emotion, and action form one system.

The eight principal karmas include knowledge-obscuring karma, perception-obscuring karma, feeling-producing karma, deluding karma, life-determining karma, physique-making karma, status-determining karma, and obstructive karma.

From a philosophy-of-mind perspective, the first four are especially important:

Knowledge-obscuring karma limits what and how the soul knows.

Perception-obscuring karma limits basic awareness.

Feeling-producing karma conditions pleasure and pain.

Deluding karma distorts belief, conduct, affect, and orientation.

Obstructive karma limits capacities such as giving, gaining, enjoyment, and effort or energy.

This makes karma a theory of cognitive opacity. The soul is intrinsically capable of knowledge, but its capacities are clouded by karmic matter. The mind is not fallen because it is bodily; it is obscured because it is karmically entangled through passionate activity.

11. Emotion, passion, and affective cognition

Umāsvāti’s theory of passion is central. Passions, kaṣāyas, include anger, pride, deceitfulness, and greed in later Jain classifications, and the Tattvārthasūtra also connects intense dispositions caused by passions with conduct-deluding karma.

The Jain view is not that emotions are merely subjective feelings. They are ontologically consequential states. They bind karma, distort cognition, obstruct conduct, and extend transmigration. In modern terms, this is an affective-cognitive theory of bondage: emotion shapes attention, belief, perception, agency, and future embodiment.

The text’s treatment of meditation makes this even clearer. Internal austerities include expiation, reverence, service, study, renunciation, and meditation. Stoppage of karma is effected through control, carefulness, virtue, contemplation, endurance, and conduct.

Not all meditation is liberating. Sorrowful meditation includes repeatedly thinking about the removal of disagreeable objects. Cruel meditation concerns injury, falsehood, theft, and guarding possessions.

This is philosophically subtle. Mental repetition, rumination, hostile imagination, and possessive fixation are not neutral inner events. They are karmically and ethically loaded. Umāsvāti therefore has a proto-theory of maladaptive attention: what one repeatedly attends to shapes the soul’s bondage.

12. Agency, will, and responsibility

Umāsvāti’s model is neither libertarian in a simple sense nor deterministic in a way that eliminates agency. The soul is active, but its activity is conditioned by karma. Passionate activity binds new karma. Disciplined conduct stops new influx. Austerity and meditation shed existing karma. This gives the system a layered account of agency.

The soul is responsible because its passions, negligence, wrong belief, and activities participate in bondage. But the soul is also conditioned because karmas obscure knowledge, perception, feeling, effort, and conduct. The path is therefore transformative: the agent begins under constraint and gradually becomes freer as it reduces the causes of bondage.

The Jain model resembles a moral-psychological compatibilism: the embodied subject is causally conditioned, but those conditions can be modified through right view, right knowledge, right conduct, restraint, meditation, and passionlessness.

Agency is also not merely mental. It operates through body, speech, and mind. This triadic model is important. A purely internalist philosophy of mind might treat thought as private and action as external. Umāsvāti treats bodily action, speech, and mental activity as parallel forms of yoga, each capable of karmic consequence.

13. Personal identity and the persistence of the self

Umāsvāti’s philosophy of mind presupposes a robust theory of personal identity. The soul persists through changing modes and embodiments. This is enabled by the Jain substance-mode ontology: substances endure while their modes arise and perish.

This differs from psychological-continuity theories, where memory or character continuity may be treated as the basis of identity. For Umāsvāti, memory and recognition are functions within an already enduring soul. They do not create the self’s identity. The self is a persistent substance whose cognitive modes vary.

It also differs from Buddhist no-self theories. The Buddha’s non-self argument, as summarized by the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, reasons from the impermanence of the five psychophysical aggregates to the denial of a permanent self; it treats mind as a bundle of transient mental events rather than a persisting bearer of cognition, feeling, and volition. Umāsvāti takes the opposite route: cognition requires a conscious substance, and that substance can persist through karmic change.

This also means the Jain self is not merely a human self. Jainism classifies living beings by senses, from one-sensed beings upward, and even one-sensed beings possess minimal conscious activity such as tactile experience. Some five-sensed beings have manas, considered by some Jain authors a sixth sense.

The Jain theory of mind is thus wider than human psychology. Consciousness belongs to jīva as such, not only to rational humans.

14. Liberation as perfected consciousness

The path to liberation begins with right view, right knowledge, and right conduct. The metaphysics of mind is inseparable from this path. The problem is not that the soul lacks consciousness; the problem is that its consciousness is obstructed.

Liberation proceeds through stopping new karmic influx and shedding old karmas. Stoppage, saṃvara, is the obstruction of influx; shedding, nirjarā, is the falling away of karmas after fruition or through disciplined practice.

The culmination is kevala-jñāna, omniscience. Tattvārthasūtra 1.29 says omniscience extends to all substances and all their modes simultaneously.

This is the apex of Umāsvāti’s philosophy of mind. The fully purified mind is not a better empirical mind. It is not sharper sense perception, better inference, or more reliable memory. It is the soul’s unobstructed knowledge of all reality, no longer dependent on senses, manas, testimony, or sequential attention.

Strictly speaking, the liberated soul no longer needs “mind” in the narrow manas sense. Manas is an embodied instrument. Pure upayoga remains. Thus, if we ask what the highest mind is for Umāsvāti, the answer is paradoxical but precise: the highest mind is not manas at all; it is unoccluded consciousness.

15. Position within Indian philosophy of mind

Umāsvāti’s view occupies a distinctive middle position among Indian theories.

Against Cārvāka materialism, it denies that consciousness is a bodily product. Cārvāka thinkers treated consciousness as emerging from material elements and the self as nothing but a conscious body. Umāsvāti instead treats consciousness as the defining mark of a non-material soul.

Against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, the difference is subtler. Nyāya accepts a self, body, senses, objects, cognition, and manas, but it treats consciousness as an adventitious quality of the self during embodiment. Umāsvāti treats upayoga as the defining characteristic of jīva. Nyāya’s manas is an internal organ mediating internal perception and coordinating sense information, which converges with Jain instrumentalism, but the ontological status of consciousness differs.

Against Buddhist no-self theories, Umāsvāti insists on an enduring conscious substance. Buddhism can analyze experience into aggregates and momentary events; Jainism insists that cognitive, karmic, and moral continuity require a persisting jīva.

Against Advaita Vedānta, especially later Śaṅkara, Jainism rejects the identity of individual self and nondual Brahman. Advaita identifies ātman with Brahman and develops a metaphysics of pure nondual consciousness; Umāsvāti’s Jainism remains pluralist, with many distinct souls and real non-soul substances.

Against Sāṃkhya, Jainism agrees that consciousness and matter are distinct, but does not make the conscious principle a merely passive witness. Jain souls undergo modes, act, bind karma, and must purify themselves. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that, unlike Sāṃkhya, Jainism allows modifications not only in matter but also in selves.

Historically, this makes Umāsvāti one of the early systematic architects of a non-Buddhist, non-Vedāntic, non-materialist philosophy of consciousness. Later Jain logic and epistemology developed in debate with other Indian traditions, especially Nyāya and Buddhist epistemology.

16. Comparison with Western philosophy of mind

The closest Western analogies are useful, but none is exact.

Aristotle

Aristotle’s hylomorphism treats soul as the form or actuality of the living body, and perception as involving the acquisition of perceptible forms. He seeks a middle path between reductive materialism and Platonic separability.

Umāsvāti shares Aristotle’s refusal to reduce living cognition to inert matter, and both thinkers analyze living beings through graded capacities. But the metaphysics differs sharply. Aristotle’s soul is generally the form of a living body; Umāsvāti’s jīva is an independent conscious substance that can transmigrate and exist apart from a given body. For Aristotle, the soul-body relation is form-matter; for Umāsvāti, it is conscious substance entangled with material and karmic conditions.

Plato and late antique soul traditions

Umāsvāti is closer to Platonic and late antique soul traditions than to Aristotle in affirming transmigration, moral purification, and the separability of soul from body. But he differs from many Western soul theories by giving karma a quasi-material mechanism and by treating consciousness as distributed across many forms of life, not only human rational souls.

Descartes

Descartes defines mind by thinking and body by extension: the nature of mind is to think, while body is extended in length, breadth, and depth.

Umāsvāti resembles Descartes in making consciousness central to the self and in refusing to identify mind with body. But the differences are decisive.

Descartes’s mind is unextended; Umāsvāti’s soul has spatial pradeśas and expands or contracts with embodiment.

Descartes’s mind-body dualism is framed by the contrast between thinking substance and extended substance; Umāsvāti’s framework is jīva, ajīva, karma, activity, passion, and liberation.

Descartes’s “mind” is the thinking subject; Umāsvāti’s manas is not the subject but an internal instrument.

Descartes faces the problem of how immaterial mind and extended body interact; Umāsvāti explains interaction through coextension, karmic matter, bodily and mental activity, and passion-driven bondage.

So Umāsvāti is a substance dualist, but not a Cartesian dualist.

Brentano and intentionality

Modern philosophy often treats intentionality as the mark of the mental: mental states are “about” or directed toward things. The Stanford Encyclopedia describes intentionality as the power of minds and mental states to represent, be about, or stand for things, and notes Brentano’s thesis that intentionality is the hallmark of the mental.

Upayoga converges with this because it is active, object-involving, attentive consciousness. But it is not merely intentionality. It is the ontological mark of the soul. The Jain criterion of mentality is not just “aboutness” but conscious manifestation, divided into perception and knowledge, subject to karmic obstruction, and capable of omniscience.

Brentano’s theory is primarily descriptive-phenomenological and psychological. Umāsvāti’s is metaphysical, ethical, and soteriological.

Physicalism and functionalism

Modern physicalism says, in broad slogan form, that everything is physical. Functionalism identifies mental states by their causal roles in a cognitive system.

Umāsvāti rejects physicalism because consciousness is not physical. But he is not blind to functional organization. Senses, mind, body, speech, passions, karmic states, and conduct all play causal roles. His view is therefore functionally articulated but not functionalist. The function of a state does not make it mental. It becomes part of mental life only because it serves or obstructs the upayoga of a jīva.

Panpsychism

Panpsychism is the view that mentality is fundamental and ubiquitous in the natural world.

Umāsvāti is not a straightforward panpsychist. Matter as such is not conscious. Ajīva lacks the mark of soul. But Jainism greatly expands the range of conscious beings beyond ordinary modern biological assumptions. One-sensed beings have minimal conscious activity, and Jain texts can treat plants and elemental bodies as inhabited by souls under certain conditions.

So Jainism is not “everything is conscious.” It is closer to radical sentient pluralism: consciousness belongs wherever there is jīva, and jīvas may inhabit forms of embodiment far wider than ordinary human-centered psychology allows.

Contemporary embodied cognition

Contemporary embodied approaches often criticize the idea that mind is an isolated inner theater. Umāsvāti would agree that ordinary cognition is not disembodied: senses, manas, body, speech, respiration, material supports, and environmental causes matter. But he would reject the conclusion that consciousness is produced by embodiment. Embodiment is the condition of ordinary cognition, not the source of consciousness itself.

17. The reconstructed system in explicit theses

Here is the extracted philosophy of mind in compact form.

1. The subject thesis. The fundamental subject of experience is jīva, not body, brain, or manas.

2. The consciousness thesis. The defining mark of jīva is upayoga, active or attentive consciousness.

3. The dual-mode thesis. Conscious manifestation has two principal forms: jñāna, determinate knowledge, and darśana, indeterminate perception or awareness.

4. The instrument thesis. Senses and manas are instruments of embodied cognition; they are not the subject.

5. The mediation thesis. Sense-based and linguistic cognition are indirect because they depend on instruments and external conditions.

6. The directness thesis. The highest knowledge is direct because it arises in the soul without sensory mediation.

7. The reflexivity thesis. Knowledge is self-manifesting as well as object-manifesting; it does not require an infinite regress of second-order cognitions.

8. The obstruction thesis. Cognitive limitation is caused by karmic obstruction, especially knowledge-obscuring, perception-obscuring, deluding, and obstructive karmas.

9. The affective thesis. Passions such as anger, pride, deceit, and greed are not peripheral emotions; they are central causes of bondage and distortion.

10. The activity thesis. Body, speech, and mind are karmically significant channels of activity.

11. The embodiment thesis. Ordinary cognition requires material and psychical supports, including body, senses, mind, and karmic conditions.

12. The persistence thesis. The self persists as a substance through changing modes, bodies, and karmic states.

13. The plurality thesis. There are many conscious selves, not one universal consciousness.

14. The purification thesis. Ethical discipline, restraint, meditation, and right knowledge are methods of cognitive purification.

15. The liberation thesis. Liberation is the complete removal of karmic obstruction, culminating in omniscient knowledge of all substances and modes.

18. Philosophical strengths

The first strength is integration. Umāsvāti does not isolate perception, belief, emotion, action, ethics, and liberation into separate domains. He treats them as one system. This is philosophically powerful because it explains why false belief, passion, negligence, and conduct affect cognition.

The second strength is its non-reductive account of embodiment. The body and mind are not dismissed as illusions, but neither are they mistaken for the conscious subject. This avoids both crude materialism and crude disembodied spiritualism.

The third strength is the distinction between manas and consciousness. Many philosophical confusions arise from treating “mind” as both the subject and an instrument. Umāsvāti avoids this. The soul is the subject; manas is an instrument.

The fourth strength is its theory of affective distortion. Passions do not merely accompany cognition; they deform it. That idea converges with contemporary concerns about motivated reasoning, attention, trauma, rumination, and affective bias, even though Umāsvāti’s explanatory framework is karmic rather than psychological or neuroscientific.

The fifth strength is its graded epistemology. Cognition ranges from minimal awareness to sense-based knowledge, memory, recognition, inference, scriptural cognition, extraordinary direct knowledge, and omniscience. That gives the system a broad architecture of mind rather than a narrow theory of perception.

19. Philosophical difficulties

The first difficulty is the empirical status of karmic matter. Within Jain metaphysics, karma is subtle matter and functions lawfully. From a modern scientific standpoint, this is not empirically established. A contemporary philosopher can reconstruct its functional role as a theory of cognitive-affective conditioning, but that translation is not identical to the traditional claim.

The second difficulty is the inclusion of clairvoyance, telepathy, and omniscience as knowledge types. These are integral to the Jain system, but they are not accepted within standard contemporary epistemology. One can analyze their conceptual role without accepting their existence.

The third difficulty is interaction. Jainism has more resources than Cartesian dualism because it posits spatially extended souls, karmic matter, and coextensive bondage. Still, the question remains: how exactly does non-material jīva interact with material karma? Jainism answers through its own categories, but the answer will not satisfy every metaphysical framework.

The fourth difficulty is translation. Upayoga is not exactly “consciousness,” “attention,” “experience,” “cognition,” or “intentionality.” Each translation captures part of it and loses part of it. A rigorous reading must preserve the Sanskrit conceptual network rather than forcing it into modern terms too quickly.

20. Final assessment

Umāsvāti deserves a major place in the global history of philosophy of mind. He articulates, centuries before early modern European philosophy, a systematic account of consciousness as the defining feature of the self; a distinction between the conscious subject and the internal mind-instrument; a theory of perception, knowledge, error, memory, recognition, and inference; a model of affective and ethical distortion; a theory of embodied cognition; and a soteriology of perfected awareness.

His view converges with Western dualisms in treating consciousness as irreducible, with Aristotle in taking embodied capacities seriously, with Brentano and phenomenology in emphasizing active conscious manifestation, and with contemporary embodied cognition in refusing to isolate cognition from bodily and practical conditions. But it differs from all of them in its Jain commitments: many souls, karmic matter, the moral physics of passion and activity, non-instrumental omniscience, and liberation as unobstructed cognition.

The most concise reconstruction is this:

For Umāsvāti, the mind is an embodied, karmically conditioned instrument of a deeper conscious subject. The soul is intrinsically cognitive; ordinary mentality is that cognition refracted through senses, mind, body, speech, passion, and karma; liberation is the restoration of cognition to its unobstructed, omniscient form.

PreviousKundakunda NextHaribhadra

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
